text:
The way we keep track of time is man made, period. There's nothing natural about standard time or DST. 12 hour clocks, AM/PM, 60 minutes per hour, 60 seconds per minute, all man-made; nothing natural about any of it. 

What would be cool is if we had dynamic clocks that shifted every day so that sunrise and sunset were the same time throughout the year. That would be as close as we can get to natural, but that means minutes would fly by faster in the winter and slow down in the summer. Upside, sunrise at 6am and sunset at 6pm every day then. Downside, that boring one hour meeting is gonna be a lot longer in the summer! 🤣

Ridiculous, I know, but my point is that whatever system you choose is going to have trade-offs that you won't like. We're forcing a man-made concept onto nature and it doesn't work consistently however you slice it. 

A simple solution would be to get rid of the whole concept of timezones and adjust our lives around UTC. Yes, that means that sunrise would be at a different time everywhere, but you'd get used to it once you live someplace for a while.

text:
Agents that search more, reason less.

(+ a strategy to cut token bills by 76%)

Here's a pattern that shows up in almost every agent trace: the model runs a search, gets backlinks and thirty-word snippets, and then has to go read the web itself.

That means fetching the pages, stripping the markup, and digging out the usable text. All of it happens inside the context window, billed as tokens, before any actual reasoning starts.

That work has a name: the retrieval tax.

On one query it's easy to ignore. In a task that takes several searches, the tax gets paid on every single one, because each new hop re-sends the entire growing context.

To measure it, I did a deliberately simple experiment. I took a question the model already knows from training, "What was Y2K?", so the thinking cost stays fixed and everything above it is pure retrieval overhead.

Answering from memory took about 600 tokens. That's the baseline.

A web search loop took about 3,750 tokens for one hop. Snippets were too thin to answer from, so the agent refetched and refined, and three hops climbed to roughly 28,700 tokens, 48x the baseline.

An owned index* answered in a single call at about 6,900 tokens.

*The owned index is the strategy from the hook, and the one most pipelines ignore. It's a search service that crawls and cleans pages ahead of time, stores its own processed copy of the web, and returns the full document the moment a query arrives.

The index pays the fetch-parse-extract cost once, at crawl time. The agent never pays it at all: one call in, complete document out, straight to reasoning.

That's where the 76% saving comes from. Same question, same answer, but the loop never happens because there's nothing left to refetch.

Full documents unlock one more thing: joins. A question like "which target accounts hired a data or AI lead last quarter" doesn't live in any single search result. It only exists when complete role histories and dated news articles are cross-referenced, which snippets can never support.

This isn't an argument against web search. Open search is still the right tool for discovery: finding who holds a title today, confirming something launched last week.

The takeaway is that retrieval shape should match the question. Discovery goes to open search, depth goes to the index, and each iteration stops paying for pages that were already read.

Seltz is a web index built exactly for this, with three scopes (people, news, wiki) that each return finished documents in one API call. The numbers above come from running the comparison against it.

If you're building agents that live on web data, it is worth trying.

Check this out: https://t.co/it50hGCCHG

The visual below breaks down all three strategies.

I also wrote an article on the same topic that covers all of these ideas and experiments in greater detail. Give it a read.

The article is quoted below.

And thanks to Seltz for partnering with me on this one.

text:
Time zones create a natural experiment.

People living on the western side of a time zone experience sunset by the clock about an hour later than people just across the border in the neighboring time zone—even though they may live only a few miles apart.

Giuntella and Mazzonna found that this later clock-time sunset was associated with about 19 minutes less sleep per night, along with higher rates of obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and breast cancer.

The sun did not change. The clock did.

That is exactly the direction permanent daylight saving time would push the entire country: later sunsets by the clock, darker mornings, less sleep, and greater circadian misalignment.

This is not merely a preference for “more evening light.” It is a population-level health experiment—and we already have warning signs from the time-zone borders.

Study: Giuntella & Mazzonna, Journal of Health Economics
https://t.co/YWKLFSAlBr

text:
KARPATHY’S WIKI LAYER: THE ULTIMATE LLM KNOWLEDGE BASE

Every time you ask a question the model re-reads all your raw PDFs, notes and files from scratch

Wastes tokens. Loses context. Misses connections. Gives weaker answers

Karpathy solved this with the Wiki Layer

I wrote the full practical guide on how to build it

The idea is simple but insanely powerful:

Instead of working with messy raw files every single time, let the LLM clean, structure and connect everything once into a clean, linked knowledge base

After that you never touch raw files again

What you get:

> 70-90% token savings on repeated queries
> Dramatically better answer quality and relevance
> Automatic [[wiki links]] between all your documents
> Visual knowledge graph in Obsidian
> A system that continuously grows and updates itself
> 100% local and private

The structure is dead simple:

> raw/ - your immutable source of truth (never edit manually)
> wiki/ - clean, templated Markdown the LLM actually works with
> Instructions + templates - rules for cleaning, linking, metadata and updates

You launch a dedicated structuring agent in Claude once

It does all the heavy lifting

You can read more about it in the article linked below the video

Save this video and come back to it later

text:
Caller (Sarah): My husband 31 and I 21, and our household income is about $235,000. My parents set up a 529 college savings plan for me, which paid for most of my undergraduate and graduate education. I still have about $35,000 in federal student loans, but recently my parents asked me to repay them $114,000 the full value of the 529 plan.

Jade Warshaw: What?!

George Kamel: Wait... is that what they contributed, or the account balance?

Sarah: That's the account balance.

George Kamel: So they want you to repay not only what they contributed, but all of the investment growth too?

Jade Warshaw: Was that ever the agreement?

Sarah: I don't remember it that way, but my dad is a lawyer. Before I went to college, he had me sign a promissory note.

Jade Warshaw: Oh no...

George Kamel: Have you read it as an adult?

Sarah: Yes. It says I promise to repay my parents for all sums paid toward my education, including tuition, housing, and living expenses.

Jade Warshaw: Does it mention interest?

George Kamel: It says "all sums paid," which is an interesting choice of words.

Sarah: I didn't really understand what I was signing at the time.

George Kamel: Let him take you to court. That would be one way to end a father-daughter relationship.

Shown below would be the new sunrise and sunset times in December. Thoughts?

text:
A 21-year-old guy from Shanghai spent his last $3,000 on five Mac minis.

His parents thought he had lost it.

Five tiny computers.
No apartment upgrade.
No savings left.

He did not buy them for gaming or crypto.

He uses them to run private AI agents for small accounting firms:
reading invoices, searching contracts, drafting reports, answering routine client emails.

The firms pay $450/month because their documents never leave his local machines.

> 8 firms x $450 = $3,600/month
> $3,000 hardware cost
> paid back before the second rent bill

A cloud GPU setup would have cost him 10x more.

A normal AI SaaS would keep charging him every time a client ran another workflow.

Five Mac minis are not a data center.

But for a 21-year-old with no investor and no runway, they are five employees that work while he sleeps.

It’s stupid!

Don’t mess with Mother Nature!

Permanent Daylight Savings Time is coming. 

I made this map, as there are no good up to date graphics online showing all the places on permanent DST. BC, Alberta, Saskatchewan, and the Yukon, are all on permanent DST. Canada is shifting all the timezones. 

If Trump gets the Sunshine Protection Act passed, Ontario and Quebec will be in purple, and the entire US will shift colors, Alaska will be red, California, Oregon, Washington, will be green, and so on...

text:
Everywhere is going to be on permanent daylight savings time soon. BC, Alberta, Saskatchewan, The Yukon are all on permanent DST now. Same with a few places in the US, like western Arizona and western Hawaii. All permanent DST. 

As you can from the map I made below, the timezones are already starting to shift drastically. 20 US States have already passed legislation make DST permanent. It just requires Trump to change the federal law. 

Plus, all US states can make ST permanent now, but none have for over 60 years. Permanent DST is coming to all of North America.

text:
Not true. Any US state can get rid of switching clocks right now. And have permanent standard time. But none have for more than 60 years. 

20 US States have passed laws to make Daylight Savings Time permanent, but they can't switch until Trump changes the law. Most of Canada is on permanent DST. Ontario and Quebec also passed legislation to make DST permanent but it's waiting on the US to do it first.

quote.text:
Twice a year, Americans lose sleep, disrupt their routines, and pay the price for a practice that’s outlived its original purpose. That’s why I voted for the Sunshine Protection Act, which would make Daylight Savings Time permanent year-round. Eliminating the switch would increase worker safety, lessen the spike in heart attacks and strokes that follow the spring forward transition, and even reduce energy use and utility bills. This bill offers a simple fix - no more clock changes, more usable daylight, and a system that works better for working families and rural communities.

Watch here: https://t.co/bK8SYZMvDF

text:
Gila sih, baru nyobain klink AI 3.0 Turbo. bisa se real gini ey.

Ini prompt nya.

Prompt - Main Subject:
Young Indonesian woman, early 20s, loose knit sweater, straight-leg jeans, hair down and slightly tousled, curious relaxed expression.

Location:
Small residential street corner near her home, mid-morning. Low garden walls, potted flowers, a parked delivery bicycle nearby, quiet street with mild morning haze. No commercial signage, no crowds.

Visual Style:
Ultra-realistic documentary realism, unscripted candid feeling, natural soft daylight.

Camera Style:
Early 2000s DV camcorder, handheld sway, autofocus struggling on distant delivery bike, faded soft contrast, mild sensor noise. No stabilization. ratio 16:9.

Timeline (15 sec, each slot = 2 compressed beats):
00:00–00:03 → She stands by the gate, then checks her phone briefly for the time.
Scene 1 → A delivery bicycle approaches in the distance; she waves, saying "여기요!" ("Over here!").
Scene 2→ She receives a small paper bag, then peeks inside curiously.
Scene 3→ She thanks the delivery person with a small bow, then turns back toward the house.
Scene 4→ She looks at camera saying "아직 따뜻하다, 좋아" ("It's still warm, nice"), smiling as she walks inside.
Audio:
Bicycle bell, distant birds, faint wind, paper bag rustling. Her dialogue as noted above. No music.
Goal: A small, genuine everyday errand moment warm, ordinary, believable.

text:
𝗖𝗮𝘃𝗲𝗮𝘁 𝗩𝗲𝗻𝗱𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿 - “𝗟𝗲𝘁 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿 𝗕𝗲𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲”

Let me start with this: 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗱𝗮 𝗶𝘀 𝗮 𝗯𝗲𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗳𝘂𝗹 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝘂𝗻𝘁𝗿𝘆, not a corrupt government department, an opportunistic political party, or a former Immigration Minister who introduced the Canadian Experience Class (CEC) and thereby, created a pathway that allowed individuals with just one year of Canadian work experience on their PGWP to apply for permanent residence.

𝗦𝗼, 𝗹𝗲𝘁’𝘀 𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗱𝗮 𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 and instead talk about what I believe is 𝗼𝗻𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝘂𝗯𝗹𝗶𝗰 𝗽𝗼𝗹𝗶𝗰𝘆 𝗳𝗮𝗶𝗹𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗶𝗻 𝗖𝗮𝗻𝗮𝗱𝗶𝗮𝗻 𝗵𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆:

Mass Unvetted Immigration driven by:

👉 Former Canadian Immigration Ministers
👉 Canadian Public colleges partnering with Private colleges
👉 Canadian registered businesses and big-box retailers
👉 Canadian Provincial ministries
👉 Canadian municipalities (Urban & Rural)

While many sellers proudly rely on “Caveat Emptor” (“Let the Buyer Beware”), as a former buyer, I believe “Caveat Venditor” (“Let the Seller Beware”) is far more appropriate for mass immigration and the ripple effects we are witnessing today.

𝗦𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗰𝘁 𝗟𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆: A seller must be held responsible for defective products or services that cause harm. Government-approved public-private partnerships played a central role in expanding the International Student Program. If institutions failed to meet expected standards, why have the DLIs involved not faced stronger regulatory consequences?

𝗚𝗼𝗼𝗱 𝗙𝗮𝗶𝘁𝗵: Both parties are expected to act honestly and fairly in a commercial transaction. Many students paid $40,000 or more for diplomas that they now believe have no value. They enrolled through partnerships between public and private colleges after governments approved these institutions and offered PGWP eligibility. They were told these were legitimate institutions and academic programs to trust.

𝗨𝗻𝗷𝘂𝘀𝘁 𝗘𝗻𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗵𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁: Where one party unfairly benefits at another’s expense, restitution becomes a legitimate question. Will the provincial governments that approved DLIs for these private colleges or the federal government that approved study permits tied to PGWP eligibility under Public Private Partnerships ever compensate students who invested $40,000 in programs they believe failed to deliver what was represented? Without the immigration pathway that many relied upon, many argue those diplomas are not even worth the paper they are printed upon.

Governments collected 𝗺𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝘃𝗲 𝗲𝗰𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗶𝗰 𝗴𝗮𝗶𝗻𝘀, Public & Private Colleges raked in 𝘁𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗿𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘂𝗲, businesses gained access to 𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗮𝗽 𝗹𝗮𝗯𝗼𝘂𝗿, housing demand increased 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘁𝘆 𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘂𝗲, and institutions prospered on 𝗳𝗮𝗹𝘀𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗶𝘀𝗲𝘀. Meanwhile, thousands of young people invested their savings, only to face changing policies, uncertain futures and rational hate.

𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝘂𝘆𝗲𝗿 𝗰𝗲𝗿𝘁𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗹𝘆 𝗵𝗮𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝘀𝗽𝗼𝗻𝘀𝗶𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗶𝗲𝘀. 𝗕𝘂𝘁 𝘀𝗼 𝗱𝗼𝗲𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝘀𝗲𝗹𝗹𝗲𝗿. A seller should not escape accountability for practices that allegedly mislead buyers or create unreasonable expectations.

👉 Mass recruitment.
👉 Mass promises.
👉 Mass profits.
👉 Mass disappointment.

Yet somehow, the seller claims no responsibility because 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗱𝗶𝘀𝗰𝗹𝗮𝗶𝗺𝗲𝗿 𝗸𝗲𝗽𝘁 𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿 𝘁𝗶𝗺𝗲.

text:
HOUSE PASSES BILL TO MAKE DAYLIGHT SAVING TIME PERMANENT

The House on Tuesday passed a bill that would make Daylight Saving Time permanent, advancing a yearslong effort to end the practice of changing the clocks twice a year.

The Sunshine Protection Act passed the lower chamber with strong bipartisan support in a 308-171 vote and will now head to the Senate. The upper chamber previously passed a similar measure in 2022, but the House didn’t take up the proposal at the time. President Donald Trump has also backed the idea of making Daylight Saving Time permanent, calling on Congress to “push hard for more daylight at the end of the day.”

Full article:
https://t.co/u8AVIc6JPq

text: Daylight savings time means waking up one hour early year round.  It means waking up in the dark more often and traveling to work or school during darker mornings.  It means more snow and ice during winter mornings because snow and ice will have less time to melt off. Evening activities will have more sunlight but morning activities will have less.

value.string_value: We could soon be observing year-round daylight saving time – again.
Why permanent daylight saving time has failed in the US before

text:
5 ways the optimal outdoor light window shifts across six months at mid-latitudes:

1. Daylight saving time advances clocks by an hour without advancing the sun - a fixed noon routine can land up to 60 minutes away from the actual UV peak by midsummer, and that gap alone changes what the session delivers.

2. The UVB window for meaningful vitamin D synthesis narrows sharply through fall - at 40°N it can span most of the midday hours in June but compress to an hour or two by late fall, making an off-center appointment far more costly than it was in summer.

3. Solar noon elevation drops from roughly 73° to 27° between June and December at 40°N - UVB travels through far more atmosphere at that low angle, cutting effective intensity to a fraction of the summer value even when you are out at the same clock hour.

4. Sunrise moves roughly 90 minutes earlier by midsummer at mid-latitudes - a fixed 8 a.m. outdoor habit sits about 45 minutes after sunrise in December but nearly 2.5 hours after sunrise in June, sliding well past the window where morning light does its strongest circadian work.

5. These shifts compound without visible feedback - a routine that was well-timed in one season can sit nearly two hours outside the optimal window six months later, accruing a quiet deficit in both UV output and circadian signal strength long before sleep or energy noticeably suffers.

author.name: Senator Patty Murray
author.description: U.S. Senator, Washington state | @SenateApprops Vice Chair | Working every day to help people and solve problems

text:
Today is Daylight Savings, and once again we all lost an hour of sleep.  No one likes it. Changing the time twice a year is just stupid.  So why isn’t it gone?  I’ll explain:  

- Last year the US Senate voted to pass the bipartisan “Sunshine Protection Act,” to make Daylight saving time the permanent time in the US. 
- When it was time to vote on it in the House, to move it to Biden’s desk, the House could not agree on whether we to make Daylight Saving time the actual time or Standard time the actual time. 
- This led to the bill never being voted on in the House. 
- On March 2 of this year the Senate reintroduced the bill. 

Let’s hope it passes into law this time!

text:
Twice a year, Americans lose sleep, disrupt their routines, and pay the price for a practice that’s outlived its original purpose. That’s why I voted for the Sunshine Protection Act, which would make Daylight Savings Time permanent year-round. Eliminating the switch would increase worker safety, lessen the spike in heart attacks and strokes that follow the spring forward transition, and even reduce energy use and utility bills. This bill offers a simple fix - no more clock changes, more usable daylight, and a system that works better for working families and rural communities.

> LLMs waste insane amounts of tokens because they have to re-read all your raw files from scratch every single conversation
> Instead, you let the LLM process everything once: clean the data, structure it into clean Markdown, and automatically create smart links between concepts
> You get a clean wiki/ folder that Obsidian turns into a beautiful visual knowledge graph with instant search and connections
> After setup you never touch raw files again - you just tell the model “work with my Wiki database”
> Result: up to 70-90% token savings, much higher answer quality, automatic relationships between notes, and a system that grows with you over time

The House just passed a bill to make Daylight Saving Time permanent.

No more changing the clocks twice a year.

Some people are celebrating. 
Others are calling it government control over your sleep schedule and circadian rhythm.

They tried this experiment in 1974 and it failed miserably — people struggled with dark winter mornings, kids going to school in the dark, etc.

So why push it again now just days after CERN shut down?

Another coincidence... or is this part of a much larger agenda to control time, sleep, and human biology?

L18: # Russia Time Change Finalized
L22: Published 20-Apr-2011
L24: The Russian parliament has passed legislation to abolish the yearly switch between standard time and daylight saving time (DST).
L26: *The hands of this Kremlin clock will not be turned back this fall.
L26: *
L28: The bill entitled "On the Calculation of Time" was approved by the Duma on Tuesday, April 19, 2011.
L28: The vote was a mere formality as Russian president Dmitry Medvedev had already proclaimed the end of "winter time" in February 2011.
L30: ## Two hours ahead of the sun
L32: Russia moved its clocks one hour forward to daylight saving time on March 27, 2011.
L32: According to the new legislation this is now the country's new standard time.
L32: As a result, all of Russia's nine time zones will be permanently two hours ahead of *mean solar time* , which corresponds to the position of the sun at a given longitude.
L34: ## Comprehensive reform
L36: The cancellation of DST in Russia is part of a comprehensive time reform that has so far included abolishing two time zones .
L36: It is also expected that the Irkutsk and Krasnoyarsk time zones will be merged in the near future .
L36: The time reform in Russia has had repercussions in several former republics of the Soviet Union: both the Ukraine and Belarus are currently considering abolishing DST."},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_in_Russia","title":"Time in Russia - Wikipedia","snippet":"L13: There are 11 time zones in Russia , which currently observe times ranging from UTC+02:00 to UTC+12:00.
L13: Daylight saving time (DST) has not been used in Russia since 26 October 2014.
L13: From 27 March 2011 to 26 October 2014, permanent DST was used.
L13: About 63% of the Russian population live in MSK (UTC+03:00).

L37: [ edit ]
L39: Prior to 2011, Russia moved its clocks backward and forward on the same annual cycle as Europe.
L39: On 27 March 2011, clocks were advanced as usual, but they did not go back on 30 October 2011, effectively making Moscow Time UTC+04:00 permanently.
L39: [ 5 ] On 26 October 2014, following another change in the law, the clocks in most of the country were moved back one hour, but summer daylight saving time was not reintroduced; Moscow Time returned to UTC+03:00 permanently.

L14: # Introduction: Time’s Up
L16: Eliot Borenstein
L18: 1 Scheduling Conflicts On or about December 1991, the normal course of time in Russia stopped.
L18: To put it more precisely, the normal course of time in the Soviet Union ended along with the country itself, leaving Russia and the other fourteen successor states to reset their clocks for a new era.
L18: Not literally, of course, although the Russian state would occasionally tinker with the metaphorical timepieces established by the USSR.
L18: Among the many aspects of the Soviet Union inherited by the Russian Federa - tion was a voluntarist approach to time.
L18: In 1930, all clocks in the Soviet Union were moved ahead one hour, a phenomenon known as “decree time” (“Stat’ia No. 362”).
L18: Though the change was motivated by practicality, what could be more symbolic of the USSR’s status as the harbinger of the future?
L18: It was rolled back in the Russian Federation in the mid-1980s, only to be restored just one month after the December accords that wiped the Soviet Union from the face of the earth (“Postanovlenie Sovmina”; “Postanovlenie Pravitel’stva RF”).
L18: The Soviet Union also had daylight saving time, which was made permanent in Russia in 2011 (i.e., the country sprang forward without falling back), and then abolished in 2014 (falling back, never to spring forward again) (“Russian Clocks Go Back for Last Time”)."}]},{"toolUsageCardId":"2061c661-e224-4e38-b47d-2085f8ca9832","webResults":[{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Time_in_Mexico","title":"Time in Mexico - Wikipedia","snippet":"L56: [ 25 ] [ 26 ] The government of the Federal District issued a decree attempting to also discontinue observing daylight saving time, but the Supreme Court ruled that only Congress had the power to decide on the subject.

L24: The period of the year from spring to fall, when clocks in most parts of the United States are set one hour ahead of standard time, is called daylight saving time, and its beginning and ending dates and times are set by federal law.
L24: The human biological clock is regulated by the timing of light and darkness, which then dictates sleep and wake rhythms.
L24: In daily life, the timing of exposure to light is generally linked to the social clock.
L24: When the solar clock is misaligned with the social clock, desynchronization occurs between the internal circadian rhythm and the social clock.
L24: The yearly change between standard time and daylight saving time introduces this misalignment, which has been associated with risks to physical and mental health and safety, as well as risks to public health.
L24: In 2020, the American Academy of Sleep Medicine (AASM) published a position statement advocating for the elimination of seasonal time changes, suggesting that evidence best supports the adoption of year-round standard time.
L24: This updated statement cites new evidence and support for permanent standard time.
L24: It is the position of the AASM that the United States should eliminate seasonal time changes in favor of permanent standard time, which aligns best with human circadian biology.
L24: Evidence supports the distinct benefits of standard time for health and safety, while also underscoring the potential harms that result from seasonal time changes to and from daylight saving time.

text:
We’ve been sold a story that seedless fruits are just a modern luxury designed to make snacking easier. But the reality is far more concerning. When you remove the seed, you remove the cycle of life. You remove your innate ability to take that fruit, plant it in your own soil, and feed your family for seasons to come. 

Seedless fruits aren’t a convenience—they’re a slow, quiet removal of our ability to grow our own food. By breeding and engineering the seeds out of our produce, the corporate agricultural industry ensures we stay completely reliant on them for our survival. We become permanent consumers instead of creators. We are trading our ultimate food sovereignty for a slightly less messy kitchen counter.

It’s time to start paying attention to what’s missing from our plates.

Take back your power. Seek out organic, heirloom varieties. Support local farmers who grow natural, seeded fruits. Start saving your seeds and planting them in your own backyard.

~ Ronnie Leonard

✨🙌🏽💫

text:
Could the US stop changing clocks? ⏰ The House of Representatives is voting on a bill that would make daylight saving time permanent across most of the U.S. If it passes, it would need to be approved by the Senate before going to the President. 

If the Sunshine Protection Act became law and a state chose to remain on daylight saving time, winter mornings would be darker, with sunrises pushed an hour later. On Jan. 15, sunrise and sunset times would look something like this:

In Miami, the sun would rise at 8:09 a.m. and set at 6:51 p.m. In Boston, sunrise would happen at 8:10 a.m., with sunset at 5:36 p.m. Farther west, Kansas City would see the sun rise at 8:35 a.m. and set at 6:19 p.m. In Billings, Montana, sunrise would occur at 8:51 a.m., with sunset at 5:56 p.m. In Los Angeles, sunrise would happen just before 8 a.m. and sunset shortly after 6 p.m. PT.

Daylight saving time is currently observed by most of the U.S. from March into November. In 2026, clocks sprang forward on March 8. Clocks are scheduled to fall back on Nov. 1, when most of the country returns to standard time.

text:
The science strongly supports ending the twice-yearly clock changes.

Where the debate begins is which time should be permanent.

Most sleep and circadian experts favor permanent standard time, not permanent daylight saving time, because morning light is the strongest cue for aligning our biological clocks.

As @hubermanlab has noted, we often confuse what feels good (later sunsets) with what is biologically optimal (earlier morning light).

An effort to make daylight saving time permanent is one step closer to becoming law.

The House overwhelmingly passed the Sunshine Protection Act, a bipartisan bill that would allow states to observe daylight saving time year-round.

The measure now heads to the Senate, where its future remains uncertain. President Trump has said he supports ending the seasonal clock changes and is expected to sign the bill if it reaches his desk.

text:
A lot of people debate Daylight Saving Time vs Standard time. Right now, it’s being debated in the government with many advocating for permanent daylight time (one hour ahead of our natural system). 

I’m not taking sides, but am here to present you with facts about what permanent daylight time would mean, and the alternatives. The times listed are for Greensboro, NC. 

——-

Below I will list 4 scenarios:
- One, the system we currently have where we switch clocks twice a year.
- Another, where we stay on standard time.
- Another, where we stay on daylight time.
- Another, which is a 30 min compromise. 

You'll see they all have their pros and cons. 

Current system: 
- About 4 months of 8pm sunsets April - August (latest 8:43pm)
- Summer sunrise between 6 - 6:30 am (earliest 6:01am)
- About 3 months of 5-6pm sunsets (earliest 5:04pm)
- Winter sunrise mostly between 7 - 7:30 am 
- Switch clocks forward in spring, and backward in fall

Standard Time all year (which is our natural unadjusted time): 
- No 8pm sunsets ever
- About 4 months of 7pm sunsets April - August (latest 7:43pm)
- Summer sunrise between 5 - 5:30am (earliest 5:01am)
- About 3 months of 5-6pm sunsets (earliest 5:04pm)
- Winter sunrise mostly between 7 - 7:30 am
- This is our natural body clock 

Daylight Time all year (which is the adjusted +1 hour time we use in summer currently): 
- About 4 months of 8pm sunsets April - August (latest 8:43pm)
- Summer sunrise between 6 - 6:30 am (earliest 6:01am)
- No winter sunsets in the 5pm hour
- About 3 months of 6-7pm sunsets (earliest 6:04pm)
- Winter sunrise will be very late 8 - 8:30 am time!! 

30 minute switch and keep it: 
- Only about 2 months of 8pm sunsets June - July (latest 8:13pm)
- Summer sunrise between 5:30 - 6:00am (earliest 5:31am)
- About 3 months of 5:30pm - 6:30pm sunsets (earliest 5:34pm)
- Winter sunrise 7:30 - 8am time
- No changing of the clocks

If you keep standard time, there's a lot of "wasted daylight" between 5-7am and no late summer sunsets. 

If you keep daylight time, it's a weird world where our sun rises as late as 8:30am in the winter, but the sunset is never before 6pm. 

If you keep switching, you have the best of both worlds but have to endure an adjustment twice a year.

You can decide for yourself what's best!

text:
🚨 🚨 🚨 THE HOUSE JUST VOTED TO ERASE WINTER MORNING LIGHT FROM AMERICA — 308-117 🚨 🚨 🚨

The Sunshine Protection Act just passed the House. Backed by Trump. One chamber away from law.

THE BILL:
→ Makes Daylight Saving Time PERMANENT — no more clock changes, ever
→ Sponsor: Rep. Vern Buchanan (R-Fla.)
→ Vote: 308-117 — bipartisan, but not unanimous
→ Senate passed a version of this FOUR YEARS AGO — it died in the House
→ Now the House did the opposite in one afternoon

THE OPPOSITION:
→ Rep. Mary Gay Scanlon and Rep. Jim McGovern flagged darker winter mornings for schoolkids
→ Save Standard Time advocates warned about THIS exact scenario for years
→ Nobody debated permanent STANDARD time as the alternative on the floor

THE MATH:
→ Since the 1960s, America has changed clocks twice a year — that ends now
→ One Senate vote stands between this and Trump's desk
→ Four years of stalling → gone in a single House session

Read that again.

💀 This isn't a scheduling fix. It's a decision about what millions of American mornings look like for DECADES.
💀 Kids waiting for buses before sunrise, permanently, every winter.
💀 The last time Washington tried this — the 1970s — it got reversed within a YEAR when parents revolted.

They're showing you "no more clock changes."
They're NOT showing you that the 1970s version of this exact idea failed because of dark morning school commutes.

You don't pass a permanent time-zone shift 308-117 without a fight underneath the numbers. You pass it when the fight already happened behind closed doors and one side won.

I'll keep you updated. Turn on notifications right now.

text:
Statement of Agudath Israel of America on House Passage of Permanent Daylight Saving Time

Today the U. S. House of Representatives passed the Sunshine Protection Act by a wide majority. Agudath Israel of America and its constituents understand the popular desire to “lock the clock.” We are concerned, however, that by choosing permanent daylight saving time (DST) for the entire country it will put children at risk and affect our religious practices.

Permanent DST would mean sunrises after 9 am in some parts of the country for weeks in the winter thereby causing many of America's schoolchildren to head for school - by foot, by bicycle, or by bus - in the pre-sunrise darkness amd putting them at increased risk.

For the Orthodox Jewish community, an unintended, yet compelling, consequence of such legislation is the effect it will have on our fundamental religious practices. In Jewish law, morning prayers, and the rituals associated with them, must be recited no earlier than at specified times. The later sunrise occurs; the later synagogue services must begin. The extension of DST will create an extreme hardship on observant Jews. It would be extraordinarily difficult - if not impossible - to arrive on time for a job and will effect the start time of our schools.

The last time this happened in the 1970's the popularity of the measure dropped dramatically once Americans experienced one dark winter and Congress had to reverse itself within a year.

We are hopeful that the Senate will consider all of the effects of DST and compare them to the effects of permanent standard time or maintaining the status quo before rushing into this major change.

text:
DERRY TOWNSHIP, Pennsylvania — The mid-morning sun danced cleanly off the calm waters of Keystone Lake, casting a bright sheen over a scene that feels entirely unstuck from time.

Along the shoreline, kayakers glide across the glass-like water. Fishermen sit quietly by the causeway, casting lines in hope of a bite. Under the shade of the locust, maple, tulip, and oak trees, families crowd around picnic tables, sharing peanut butter and jelly sandwiches from home as they shake off the heavy heat of a Pennsylvania summer. 

It is a serene, almost languid tableau. It is also a testament to American resilience and our ability to reinvent ourselves, as the lake and rolling state park were built directly atop the industry that once defined Western Pennsylvania: coal.

Long before it was a haven for swimming, fishing, kayaking, camping, and sunbathing, this very dirt belonged to the Keystone Coal and Coke Company. In 1909, the company dammed the convergence of McCune and Davis runs, creating this 78-acre lake for a purely industrial purpose: supplying the massive amounts of water needed to wash bituminous coal and quench the fiery coke ovens at the nearby Salem No. 1 Mine.

The water the children splash in today once flowed gravity-fed through 2 miles of wooden pipes to fuel the region’s roaring steel industry.

Just a short walk from the sandy beach, beneath the park’s cabin area and the Hillside Campground, lies a labyrinth of abandoned, sealed-off mine tunnels. In those dark spaces, particularly the Salem No. 2 drift mine, which opened in 1938, local miners labored on their hands and knees inside coal seams just 2 to 4 feet high.

When the industrial demand cooled, Pennsylvania acquired the land and the company’s stone executive retreat in 1945, turning a private corporate playground into Keystone State Park. Today, that historic stone lodge serves as the James A. Kell Visitor Center, housing artifacts from the very miners who laid the foundation for the community.

Located in Westmoreland County, Keystone State Park is one of 124 state parks spread across Pennsylvania’s 67 counties. For generations of Pennsylvanians, these public lands have provided the setting for some of our most enduring traditions. Today, as families look for affordable ways to vacation and rediscover what is often right in their own backyards, these parks are playing an even more vital role. They are saving the family vacation.

I watched children ranging from toddlers to teenagers chase minnows, scoop up handfuls of sand, and sift through it for quartz, granite, rounded pebbles, and fragments of sandstone, shale, limestone, and bituminous coal. They picked out their favorites and carried them home in sand pails.

Dozens of families rested on blankets spread across the dune grass between the sandy beach and the lawn, taking a break from the sun as their children emptied out the treasures they had collected from the lakebed. Conspicuously absent were the devices that so often occupy both children and parents.

That absence may be common in the heart of the country, but it can seem almost inconceivable in a culture where social media increasingly shapes how everyday life is experienced and presented. There were no influencers posing on boogie boards. The children riding them were too busy mastering the balance needed to stay upright on the water. The same was true of those paddling canoes and kayaks.

In an era dominated by algorithms, notifications, and glowing screens, Pennsylvania’s state parks offer a radical alternative: a place to disconnect. Here, children aren’t hunched over tablets or lost in video games. They are outside, experiencing the physical world with their bare hands.

text:
America already made daylight saving time permanent once. In December 1973, 79% of Americans supported it and Nixon signed it into law. By February 1974, eight weeks into the experiment, support had collapsed to 42%. The policy never changed. Winter arrived.

Sunrise in Washington DC came at 8:27am. Kids left for school in total darkness, carrying flashlights and wearing reflective tape. When Congress moved to repeal, members cited the deaths of eight Florida schoolchildren in the first weeks of the change.

The energy case collapsed too. The whole point was the oil embargo, and the Department of Transportation concluded the fuel savings were too small to matter. Ten months after signing it, Congress repealed it and Ford put the country back on standard time.

There's a mechanism that makes this cycle repeat: permanent DST is only ever voted on when the sun is up. Polls run in the abstract, votes happen in warm months, and the benefit, a free hour of evening light, is easy to imagine year-round. The cost is concentrated in about 10 weeks of dark winter mornings and only becomes real after implementation. So the policy polls beautifully right up until January.

Today's vote happened in July. The 1974 law passed in December, right before the exact winter that killed it. If this clears the Senate, the first real test arrives in January 2027, when sunrise in Indianapolis comes after 9am.

Sleep researchers have watched this loop for 50 years, which is why the American Academy of Sleep Medicine, the AMA, and the National Sleep Foundation all back the opposite reform, permanent standard time. Congress just picked the one version of clock reform America has already tried, hated, and repealed.

text:
I am not a political person. The scientific community and the data and evidence overwhelmingly shows that morning sunlight is imperative for good health.

There are Republicans and Democrats both on the right side of this as seen by legislation just introduced - bipartisan calling for permanent standard time. But there are also Republicans and Democrats on the wrong side of this issue that because of either intellectual laziness or willful ignorance have voted for a bill that will remove morning sunlight for hundreds of millions of people that they are financially medically responsible for, and it will increase the risk of diabetes and cardiovascular disease as well as certain types of cancers that we have evidence for.

I am a boarded Sleep specialist, which means that I am an expert in this field.

This is not my opinion, but the position of thousands of professionals who understand the circadian rhythm and the tremendous impact that this will have hundreds of millions of people in the United States.

It is completely untenable that the supreme governing body of our country would make a decision about this without weighing the evidence. 

Making standard time permanent is literally the lowest hanging fruit that we could decide to improve the health of our country.   Instead, we have politicians going on this platform to boast that they gave more sunlight to their constituents. As if the stroke of their mighty pen could add one second to the amount of sunlight that comes from the sun.

Complete ignorance.

quote.text:
I am not a political person. The scientific community and the data and evidence overwhelmingly shows that morning sunlight is imperative for good health.

There are Republicans and Democrats both on the right side of this as seen by legislation just introduced - bipartisan calling for permanent standard time. But there are also Republicans and Democrats on the wrong side of this issue that because of either intellectual laziness or willful ignorance have voted for a bill that will remove morning sunlight for hundreds of millions of people that they are financially medically responsible for, and it will increase the risk of diabetes and cardiovascular disease as well as certain types of cancers that we have evidence for.

I am a boarded Sleep specialist, which means that I am an expert in this field.

This is not my opinion, but the position of thousands of professionals who understand the circadian rhythm and the tremendous impact that this will have hundreds of millions of people in the United States.

It is completely untenable that the supreme governing body of our country would make a decision about this without weighing the evidence. 

Making standard time permanent is literally the lowest hanging fruit that we could decide to improve the health of our country.   Instead, we have politicians going on this platform to boast that they gave more sunlight to their constituents. As if the stroke of their mighty pen could add one second to the amount of sunlight that comes from the sun.

Complete ignorance.

text:
The fight over daylight saving time is heating up after the House passed President Trump’s bill to stop Americans from changing their clocks twice a year.

Supporters say permanent daylight saving time would end disrupted schedules, give families more evening sunlight, and boost businesses across multiple industries.

But airlines are warning the change could ripple through aviation, impacting sunrise times, passenger schedules, crew and aircraft positioning, and coordination with global flight networks. Sleep experts are also pushing back, arguing permanent standard time is healthier than permanent daylight saving time because it better lines up with the body’s natural clock.

@Grady_Trimble has more.

NIGHTCAP QUESTION:

The House just passed a bill that would make daylight saving time permanent. Supporters say the time change disrupts sleep... but some want to keep it to cut down on dark winter mornings. 

Which side are you on?
